description: Expanded Industrial Troubleshooting Ontology with Root Cause Analysis
methodology: Each top-level problem category is decomposed into 6 root cause categories: Electrical, Mechanical, Communication, Environmental, Operational, and Fluid/Media

problem_types.name: Leakage (Internal & External)
problem_types.description: Fluid or gas escaping from seals, seats, gaskets, or bonnet joints. Includes internal seat leakage and external stem/flange leakage.

causes: Worn or degraded seals and packing material
Improper gland compression or packing nut torque
Loose flange bolts or fasteners
Gasket failure or material incompatibility
Stem or shaft scoring and surface damage
Misalignment between mating surfaces
Seat erosion from abrasive particles or cavitation
Excessive wear from pressure cycling
Limit switch misalignment preventing full closure
Actuator torque settings too low for complete sealing
Control signal drift or noise preventing stable shut-off
Failed solenoid coil unable to hold valve closed
Incorrect valve position feedback to PLC
Feedback error: HMI shows closed but valve is partially open
Loss of control signal defaulting valve to unsafe position
Network packet loss causing intermittent closure commands
Protocol timeout leaving valve in intermediate state
Thermal expansion/contraction of seals and metal components
Corrosion from ambient humidity, salt spray, or aggressive media
Debris buildup on sealing surfaces
Freeze-thaw cycles damaging elastomer seals
UV degradation of external packing materials
Over-torquing handwheel or actuator damaging seats
Improper installation (valve installed backwards)
Using wrong seal material for pressure/temperature rating
Frequent cycling beyond design cycle life
Operating at pressures exceeding valve rating
Abrasive particles eroding metal seats
Chemical attack on elastomer seals from process media
Cavitation damage to valve internals
High-velocity 'wire drawing' of soft metal seats
Solid particles lodged on sealing surface
Incompatible fluid viscosity affecting seal compression

problem_types.name: No Start / No Motion / Dead Unit
problem_types.description: Equipment fails to start, move, or respond to commands. Complete loss of function with no output or motion.

causes: Blown fuses or tripped circuit breakers
Phase loss in three-phase supply
Low supply voltage below motor minimum rating
Burnt motor windings or open circuit
Failed power supply module or transformer
Loose wiring connections at terminals
Broken conductor in power cable
Contactor/relay coil failure preventing motor energization
VFD internal fault or blown IGBT module
Seized bearings from lack of lubrication
Jammed internal components (e.g., impeller, rotor)
Excessive preload torque preventing rotation
Locked rotor due to external obstruction
Bent shaft or coupling misalignment
Gearbox internal damage or gear tooth breakage
Valve stuck in closed position blocking flow
Network disconnection (Modbus, Profibus, EtherCAT)
PLC output card failure or I/O module malfunction
HMI-to-PLC communication loss
Emergency stop (E-stop) triggered and not reset
Incorrect command signal format or protocol
Baud rate mismatch in serial communication
IP address conflict in networked devices
Moisture ingress causing electrical shorts
Extreme cold thickening lubricants beyond viscosity limits
Overheating causing thermal shutdown or component damage
Corrosion of electrical contacts preventing current flow
Dust/debris accumulation blocking air vents
Incorrect programming or control logic
E-stop not reset after emergency activation
Manual override engaged (mechanical lock)
Wrong voltage connected during installation
Incorrect motor rotation direction setting
Soft starter ramp time set too high
Load inertia exceeding motor starting torque
Solidified or crystallized media in pump/valve
Hydrate formation in gas lines blocking flow
Viscous fluid exceeding motor starting torque capacity
Air lock in pump suction line
Cavitation preventing prime in centrifugal pump

problem_types.name: Overheating / Thermal Issues
problem_types.description: Excessive temperature rise above normal operating range. Equipment becomes hot to touch or triggers thermal shutdown.

causes: Harmonic distortion from VFD operation
Unbalanced three-phase voltage
Voltage surges or spikes damaging insulation
Undersized wiring causing I²R losses
VFD carrier frequency set too high
High switching losses in power electronics
Loose electrical connections increasing resistance
Bearing friction from inadequate lubrication
Lack of lubrication in gearbox or rotating components
Internal rubbing or misalignment
Excessive load beyond design capacity
Worn bearings with increased friction
Coupling misalignment causing drag
Bent or unbalanced rotor
Cooling fan control failure or not receiving stop command
Temperature sensor error reporting false high readings
VFD not receiving speed reduction command from controller
Feedback loop instability causing oscillation and heat
High ambient temperature above design rating
Clogged cooling vents or air filters
Poor ventilation in control cabinet
Direct sunlight exposure on equipment
Dust accumulation on heat sink fins
Inadequate spacing between equipment in enclosure
Frequent start/stop cycles (short cycling)
Operating at low speeds without auxiliary cooling
Overloading equipment beyond rated capacity
Continuous operation at maximum load
Incorrect motor size for application
Soft starter ramp time too short causing high inrush current
High temperature process fluid
Low flow rate in pumps/compressors leading to heat buildup
High viscosity fluid causing excessive drag
Inadequate cooling water flow in heat exchangers
Fouling or scaling reducing heat transfer efficiency
Cavitation in pump creating turbulence and heat

problem_types.name: Erratic Reading / Signal Issues / Drift
problem_types.description: Sensor or instrument readings fluctuate, jump, or drift from true value. Unstable or unreliable measurement data.

causes: Electromagnetic interference (EMI) from motors or VFDs
Poor grounding or ground loops
Damaged cable shielding allowing noise coupling
Power supply noise affecting analog circuits
Inadequate filtering of sensor signal
Long cable runs without shielding (>100m)
Nearby high-voltage equipment radiating interference
Vibration loosening sensor connections
Physical damage to sensor element or probe
Clogged impulse lines in pressure transmitters
Mounting bracket flex causing sensor movement
Loose sensor housing allowing internal movement
Corrosion of connector pins
Protocol errors or packet loss in network
IP address conflict in networked sensors
Baud rate mismatch in serial communication
Signal attenuation over long cable runs
Timeout errors in Modbus/Profibus communication
Incorrect data format conversion in PLC
Temperature fluctuations affecting electronics
Humidity causing condensation on circuit boards
Chemical corrosion of terminals from process media
RFI from nearby radio transmitters
Thermal cycling causing solder joint failures
Moisture ingress into sensor housing
Improper sensor calibration or calibration drift
Wrong range or scaling configured in PLC
Sensor mounted in turbulent flow or dead zone
Sensor installed at wrong orientation
Sampling rate too fast for sensor response time
Using sensor outside its specified operating range
Coating or scaling buildup on sensor probe
Air bubbles in liquid lines affecting level sensors
Moisture in gas lines affecting gas analyzers
Media density changes affecting flow meter readings
Chemical reaction deposits on sensor surface
Particulate matter interfering with optical sensors

problem_types.name: Noise & Vibration
problem_types.description: Excessive audible noise, grinding sounds, or mechanical vibration beyond normal operating levels.

causes: Magnetic hum from unbalanced three-phase voltage
VFD switching noise (PWM frequency audible)
Loose transformer laminations vibrating
Coil buzz from unbalanced magnetic field
Harmonic resonance with mechanical structure
Misalignment between motor and pump/load
Unbalanced rotating parts (rotor, impeller, fan blade)
Worn bearings with internal play
Loose mounting bolts or foundation
Gear tooth wear or chipped teeth
Bent shaft or coupling
Friction between internal components
Worn piston rings in reciprocating compressor
Hunting or oscillating control loops causing rapid actuator movement
Erratic VFD speed commands causing speed fluctuation
Positioner instability causing valve hunting
Resonance with support structure or building frame
Acoustic reflection in piping or ductwork
Foundation settling or degradation
Nearby equipment vibration coupling into system
Operating outside the Best Efficiency Point (BEP)
Cavitation in pump creating noise and vibration
Water hammer from rapid valve closure
Surge or pulsation in compressor discharge
Excessive load causing mechanical stress
Rapid pressure changes in system
Entrained air or gas in liquid creating cavitation
Slug flow in gas lines
High velocity turbulence at bends or restrictions
Solids hitting internal components
Fluid vaporization at low pressure regions
Incompatible fluid viscosity causing friction

problem_types.name: Valve Stuck / Difficult Operation
problem_types.description: Valve unable to move, difficult to operate, or requires excessive force. Includes stuck open, stuck closed, or hard to turn.

causes: Corrosion buildup on stem or ball surface
Mineral deposits or scale preventing movement
Debris or foreign material lodged in valve
Stem/shaft scoring from wear
Packing too tight restricting stem movement
Actuator linkage misalignment or binding
Gearbox internal damage or gear jam
Bearing wear increasing friction
Actuator motor stalled from insufficient torque
Solenoid coil burnt out or failed
Limit switch stuck preventing valve movement
Control signal loss leaving valve in intermediate state
Brake release solenoid failure in electric actuator
Feedback error causing control system to stop
Network disconnection halting actuator command
Emergency stop engaged and not reset
Conflicting control signals from multiple sources
Freeze-thaw cycles causing expansion/contraction
Extreme cold making lubricant viscous
Corrosive atmosphere causing rust
Salt spray or chemical exposure
Over-torquing during manual operation
Improper installation (valve installed backwards)
Exceeding actuator torque rating
Leaving valve in intermediate position for extended time
Using wrong lubricant or no lubricant
High viscosity fluid at low temperature
Solidified or crystallized media
Abrasive particles causing friction
Chemical reaction creating deposits
Cavitation damage to sealing surfaces

problem_types.name: No Flow / No Output / No Discharge
problem_types.description: Pump, compressor, or actuator produces no output flow, pressure, or motion despite receiving input power.

causes: Impeller or rotor jam from debris
Inlet valve stuck closed
Discharge valve stuck closed
Worn impeller or rotor reducing displacement
Internal leakage path (worn seals) bypassing output
Bearing failure causing rotor contact with housing
Broken coupling preventing power transmission
Check valve malfunction blocking flow
Motor not running (see No Start category)
VFD fault preventing motor energization
Soft starter ramp incomplete or failed
Thermal overload tripped
Control signal loss to actuator
Feedback indicating false 'discharge blocked' condition
Pressure relief valve holding open due to signal error
Suction line frozen or iced
Inlet filter clogged with ice or debris
Discharge line frozen blocking output
Suction line not primed (air lock)
Inlet valve closed or throttled
Discharge valve closed or throttled
System pressure relief set too low
Pump operating below minimum speed
Incorrect pump rotation direction
Inlet media solidified or too viscous
Cavitation preventing prime
Air bubbles blocking flow path
Hydrate formation in gas lines
Debris or sediment blocking inlet strainer
Incompatible fluid damaging seals

root_cause_categories.name: Electrical
root_cause_categories.description: Issues related to power supply, wiring, control signals, and electrical components

root_cause_categories.name: Mechanical
root_cause_categories.description: Physical wear, misalignment, friction, and structural failures

root_cause_categories.name: Communication
root_cause_categories.description: Network connectivity, protocol errors, signal loss, and feedback issues

root_cause_categories.name: Environmental
root_cause_categories.description: Temperature, humidity, corrosion, and ambient condition effects

root_cause_categories.name: Operational
root_cause_categories.description: Human error, incorrect settings, improper procedures, and usage beyond design limits

root_cause_categories.name: Fluid/Media
root_cause_categories.description: Properties and contamination of process fluids, gases, and media
